name: ServiceNow, Inc.

profile.description: ServiceNow, Inc. provides cloud-based solutions for digital workflows, IT service management, IT operations, security operations, customer service, and HR service delivery. The company's Now Platform enables enterprise-wide digital transformation through workflow automation and AI-powered tools. Under CEO Bill McDermott since 2019, revenue has tripled from $3.46B to $13.3B. Recently acquired Moveworks ($2.85B) and Armis ($7.75B) to strengthen agentic AI and security capabilities. Founded in 2004, headquartered in Santa Clara, California.

innovation.label: STRONG
innovation.analysis: Track Record (3/3): Under McDermott's leadership since late 2019, ServiceNow has tripled annual revenue from $3.46B to $13.3B while transforming from an IT ticketing platform into an AI-defining enterprise software company [1][3]. The company launched Now Assist (generative AI for enterprise workflows) which reached $750M in annual contract value by Q1 2026, targeting $1.5B by year-end — representing one of the fastest enterprise AI product ramp-ups in software history [1][4]. The acquisition of Moveworks ($2.85B, Dec 2025) and Armis ($7.75B, April 2026) represent transformative capability additions in agentic AI and cybersecurity [4]. AI Deployment (2/2): Now Assist AI is shipping to enterprise customers with measurable results — ServiceNow reported saving $500K internally through Now Assist with 91% of its own service requests now AI-supported [4]. The AI Control Tower provides unified governance for AI agents, models, and workflows across enterprise environments spanning Microsoft, Google, AWS, OpenAI, and Anthropic [4]. Q1 2026 saw 16 transactions over $5M in net new ACV, nearly 80% YoY growth in large deals [1]. Market Share (2/2): ServiceNow is the dominant leader in IT service management and enterprise workflow automation, with current remaining performance obligations of $12.64B (22.5% YoY growth) demonstrating deepening customer commitment [1]. The platform serves government, financial services, healthcare, and manufacturing sectors with expanding wallet share. Product Pipeline (1/2): Agentic AI strategy is clear with AI Control Tower, autonomous agent governance, and workflow data fabric forming the next-gen platform vision [4]. However, the $10.6B in recent acquisitions (Moveworks + Armis) creates integration execution risk and pipeline uncertainty. R&D Intensity (1/1): ServiceNow invests heavily in R&D as a percentage of revenue, consistently above 15%, with specific focus on AI/ML capabilities, platform scalability, and security orchestration [1].

inspiration.label: STRONG
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): ServiceNow's purpose — 'Make the world work better for everyone' — is specific, actionable, and clearly shapes product strategy [3]. McDermott has unified all 29,000+ employees under the aspiration of becoming 'the AI Defining Enterprise Software Company of the 21st Century,' a vision that connects the company's workflow platform to a broader transformation of how organizations operate [3][4]. Strategic decisions — building AI governance tools, pursuing cross-platform integration, investing in agentic AI — trace directly to this purpose. The cause is well-articulated and lived, though the company's ~22-year history has not yet weathered the multi-decade test of a truly transformative Just Cause. Community Impact (1/2): ServiceNow's ESG management products help customers measure and report sustainability metrics, extending the company's impact through its platform. The company maintains programs for social impact and digital inclusion, though no transformative foundation or specific charitable dollar amounts were identified in public reporting. Employee Retention (1/2): McDermott has provided stable leadership since 2019, and the company's 29,187 employees operate in an enterprise software culture that benefits from strong compensation and growth trajectory. The Knowledge conference and employee culture receive positive commentary, though no specific turnover or tenure metrics are publicly available. Insider Buying (2/2): McDermott is among the highest-paid CEOs in enterprise software with significant equity-based compensation, and his public conviction about ServiceNow's trajectory — calling the 39% stock crash 'nonsense' and projecting a trillion-dollar company — demonstrates personal alignment with the long-term thesis [3]. McDermott's aggressive acquisition strategy reflects CEO-level confidence in the platform's potential.

infrastructure.label: STRONG
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on all available metrics. ServiceNow's financial profile is excellent across nearly every dimension. Q1 2026 delivered $3.77B total revenue (22% YoY growth) with subscription revenues of $3.67B and subscription gross margin of 81.5% [1]. Debt-to-equity of 20.7% is exceptionally low, reflecting a clean balance sheet with $5.2B cash against $2.4B debt. ROE of 16.1% is just above the 15% target. Revenue growth of 22.1% YoY and 24.1% 5-year CAGR both substantially exceed the 10% target. Forward P/E of 18.9 is below the 20 target, and Forward PEG of 0.92 is below the 1.0 target, representing attractive growth-adjusted valuation post-split [7]. The only weakness is profit margin of 12.6%, below the 20% target, reflecting higher operating expenses from recent acquisitions and aggressive investment in AI capabilities. Free cash flow of $5.1B demonstrates strong cash conversion despite lower GAAP margins.

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (8/9): ServiceNow is one of the most AI-immune enterprise software companies because AI adoption directly increases demand for its platform. As enterprises deploy more AI agents, they need more workflow orchestration, governance, and operational management — exactly what ServiceNow provides [4]. McDermott's insight that 'AI intelligence is commoditizing' while governance and orchestration become the bottleneck positions ServiceNow as infrastructure for the AI era, not a victim of it. The AI Control Tower — governing AI models, agents, and workflows across Microsoft, Google, AWS, OpenAI, and Anthropic environments — makes ServiceNow the system-of-record for enterprise AI operations [4]. Switching costs are extremely high (deep integration into enterprise IT, HR, security, and customer service processes), and no AI startup can replicate the 22 years of process knowledge embedded in the Now Platform. The only risk is that hyperscalers (Microsoft, Google) could build competing workflow layers, but ServiceNow's multi-cloud positioning provides protection [4]. AI Opportunity (1/1): Now Assist reached $750M ACV in Q1 2026, targeting $1.5B by year-end, making it one of the largest enterprise AI revenue streams in software [1]. The company uses AI internally (91% of service requests AI-supported) and sells AI governance tools, agentic AI orchestration, and AI-powered workflow automation to thousands of enterprise customers [4].

infinite_game.summary: ServiceNow under Bill McDermott shows strong infinite game characteristics, particularly in strategic courage and purpose-driven leadership. McDermott's vision of ServiceNow as the AI-defining enterprise software company is bold and forward-looking, supported by $10.6B in strategic acquisitions and a clear product roadmap. The 'make the world work better for everyone' purpose, while less poetic than some, is genuinely embedded in product decisions. The main limitation is that ServiceNow's 22-year history has not yet faced the kind of existential crisis that truly tests infinite game commitment.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): 'Make the world work better for everyone' is a genuine purpose that connects ServiceNow's workflow platform to a meaningful aspiration — reducing organizational friction so people can focus on meaningful work [3]. McDermott has sharpened this into becoming 'the AI Defining Enterprise Software Company of the 21st Century,' which is both ambitious and specific. Product decisions trace back to this cause: building AI governance tools (making AI work better), acquiring Armis (making security work better), and enabling agentic AI orchestration (making enterprise operations work better). However, the purpose is only 22 years old, has only been tested under one CEO transition (from John Donahoe to McDermott), and has not yet faced a crisis that required sacrificing short-term performance to maintain it.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): McDermott has provided stable, consistent leadership since late 2019, and his high-energy, people-focused management style is widely cited as culturally positive [3]. The company's 29,187 employees have grown through a period of tripling revenue without major cultural disruptions or mass layoffs. The annual Knowledge conference serves as a culture-reinforcing event. However, the integration of two large acquisitions (Moveworks' AI team and Armis's 1,800+ security employees) within six months creates organizational strain and integration risk. No specific employee tenure, voluntary turnover, or psychological safety metrics are publicly available.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): McDermott's competitive language is notably infinite-minded. Rather than positioning ServiceNow against specific competitors, he frames the company as managing the complexity that all technology creates: 'ServiceNow manages everyone else's board' [4]. His strategic relationship with Jensen Huang — jointly discussing how agentic AI will 'rewire a $50 trillion economy' — demonstrates an approach that elevates the entire ecosystem rather than seeking to destroy rivals [4]. McDermott acknowledges that 'AI intelligence is commoditizing' (implicitly validating competitors' AI capabilities) while arguing ServiceNow's differentiation is in orchestration and governance, not in building better AI models.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): ServiceNow has meaningfully evolved from an IT help desk ticketing system to an enterprise-wide platform for digital workflows, and is now transforming again into an AI orchestration and governance platform [3][4]. Each evolution expanded the addressable market by an order of magnitude. The acquisitions of Moveworks and Armis represent bold bets on adjacent capabilities. However, these pivots have been additive rather than truly existential — ServiceNow has never abandoned a core business or made a bet that threatened current revenue. The ITSM foundation remains the anchor, with new capabilities layered on top rather than replacing what came before.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (2/2): McDermott has demonstrated exceptional strategic courage through $10.6B in acquisitions (Moveworks $2.85B + Armis $7.75B) within a single year, fundamentally reshaping the company's capability set [4]. When ServiceNow stock crashed 39% amid SaaS sector fears, McDermott publicly dismissed the selloff as 'nonsense' and doubled down on the trillion-dollar company vision, demonstrating conviction over capitulation [3]. The decision to embed AI, data, security, and governance into every product and package — rather than selling them as separate purchases — sacrificed near-term revenue maximization for long-term platform value. Building an AI Control Tower that governs competitors' AI tools (Microsoft, Google, OpenAI, Anthropic) is a strategically audacious positioning move [4].

investment_thesis: ServiceNow is executing a textbook AI-era transformation, with Q1 2026 delivering $3.77B revenue (22% YoY growth), subscription gross margin of 81.5%, and Now Assist AI reaching $750M ACV — on track for $1.5B by year-end [1]. The company's positioning as enterprise AI infrastructure — not competing with AI models but governing and orchestrating them through the AI Control Tower — creates a uniquely defensible strategic position that benefits from every competitor's success [4]. With $12.64B in current RPO (22.5% growth), $5.1B FCF, and Forward PEG of 0.92, ServiceNow offers rare compounding growth at reasonable valuation. McDermott's vision of becoming the 'AI Defining Enterprise Software Company' is supported by $10.6B in strategic acquisitions (Moveworks + Armis) and a platform that integrates across Microsoft, Google, AWS, OpenAI, and Anthropic ecosystems [3][4].
thesis_continued: The primary risk is execution complexity — integrating $10.6B in acquisitions (Moveworks and Armis) while simultaneously launching agentic AI products, building the AI Control Tower, and maintaining 22%+ subscription growth requires flawless operational execution [1][4]. Profit margin of 12.6% is below the 20% target, reflecting both acquisition-related costs (Armis expected to create 75bps operating margin headwind) and heavy AI investment [1]. The trailing P/E of 56.6 suggests the market is pricing in substantial forward growth, and any deceleration in Now Assist adoption or subscription revenue could trigger significant multiple compression — the 39% stock decline earlier in 2026 demonstrates this vulnerability [3]. Geopolitical risks are live: ServiceNow stock dropped 14% when subscription revenue was impacted by the Iran war, demonstrating sensitivity to global enterprise spending disruptions [1]. Competition from Microsoft (Copilot) and Salesforce (Agentforce) in enterprise AI could pressure market share in adjacent categories.
key_catalysts: Now Assist AI targeting $1.5B ACV by year-end 2026 (doubling from $750M in Q1), with 16 deals over $5M net new ACV in Q1 alone demonstrating enterprise willingness to pay for AI workflow automation

AI Control Tower establishing ServiceNow as the system-of-record for enterprise AI governance — a category-defining product with no direct competitor at scale, positioned to benefit from every enterprise AI deployment regardless of model provider
Full-year 2026 subscription revenue guidance raised to $15.7-15.8B (22-22.5% growth), with Armis integration expected to unlock cross-selling opportunities in the $200B+ cybersecurity market
key_risks: Integration risk from $10.6B in acquisitions within 6 months (Moveworks $2.85B + Armis $7.75B) could distract management and dilute ServiceNow's platform cohesion, with Armis already expected to reduce FY2026 operating margin by 75bps and FCF margin by 200bps

Geopolitical exposure demonstrated by Q1 2026 subscription revenue hit from the Iran war and subsequent 14% stock decline — enterprise software spending is vulnerable to global conflict escalation and macroeconomic slowdown

Trailing P/E of 56.6 reflects premium valuation that assumes sustained 22%+ growth and AI monetization acceleration — the 39% stock crash earlier in 2026 on sector rotation fears shows how quickly sentiment can shift
